title_en: Building a Life of Purpose and Joy
title_id: Membangun Kehidupan yang Bertujuan dan Penuh Kegembiraan
content_en:
The happiness that al-Arifi envisions for Muslim women is not passive but active — not a state to be waited for but a life to be built. This concluding chapter brings together the threads of the preceding discussion into a vision of a purposeful, joyful Muslim woman's life: one that is oriented toward Allah, rich in relationships, productive in service, and resilient in the face of inevitable difficulties.

Purpose is one of the most powerful contributors to human wellbeing, and Islam provides the Muslim woman with a purpose of extraordinary depth: to worship Allah with her whole life, to serve as His vicegerent on earth in her particular station, and to contribute to the flourishing of her family, community, and humanity. This purpose is not diminished by the specific roles she occupies — wife, mother, daughter, professional, community member — but expressed through them. Every act performed with the intention of pleasing Allah is an act of worship; the Muslim woman who genuinely understands this can find meaning in the most ordinary tasks of daily life.

Al-Arifi discusses the importance of having concrete goals and projects — not merely the general aim of pleasing Allah but specific ambitions in the domains of religious knowledge, family life, service, and personal development. The Prophet taught that the believer should pursue excellence (itqan) in whatever they do, and this pursuit of excellence is itself a source of joy. The Muslim woman who sets learning goals — memorizing a portion of Quran, studying a particular Islamic science, developing a skill that allows her to serve her community more effectively — and then pursues these goals with discipline and determination, will experience the deep satisfaction of genuine accomplishment.

Service to others — dawah, charitable giving, supporting family and neighbors, contributing to community projects — is one of the most reliable sources of the kind of joy that does not quickly fade. The psychological research on this point aligns precisely with Islamic teaching: people who give their time, energy, and resources in service of others report higher levels of wellbeing than those who focus primarily on their own needs and pleasures. Islam's orientation toward service is not a burden but a prescription for happiness.

Al-Arifi addresses the common experience of feeling overwhelmed — particularly for Muslim women who are managing demanding roles simultaneously. He does not offer the fantasy of a life without overwhelm but rather the Islamic tools for managing it: the five daily prayers as structured pauses that reset one's perspective and priorities, the practice of delegating what can be delegated, the wisdom to distinguish between what is essential and what merely appears urgent, and the spiritual practice of surrendering what is beyond one's control to Allah with genuine trust.

The book concludes with a vision of the Muslimah who has absorbed its lessons: a woman who begins each day with gratitude and remembrance of Allah, who approaches her relationships with patience and love, who pursues her goals with determination and trust in Allah's support, who gives generously of her time and resources, who faces hardship with the serenity of one who knows it comes from the Most Wise, and who carries within her the settled knowledge that her life has an ultimate purpose and her story an ultimate ending of joy. This woman is, in al-Arifi's conviction, genuinely the happiest woman in the world — not because her circumstances are perfect, but because she has found the source of happiness that circumstances cannot reach.
content_id:
Kebahagiaan yang al-Arifi bayangkan untuk wanita Muslim bukanlah pasif melainkan aktif — bukan keadaan yang ditunggu tetapi kehidupan yang dibangun. Bab penutup ini menyatukan benang-benang dari diskusi sebelumnya menjadi visi kehidupan wanita Muslim yang bertujuan dan penuh kegembiraan: kehidupan yang berorientasi kepada Allah, kaya dalam hubungan, produktif dalam pelayanan, dan tangguh dalam menghadapi kesulitan-kesulitan yang tak terelakkan.

Tujuan adalah salah satu kontributor paling kuat bagi kesejahteraan manusia, dan Islam memberi wanita Muslim tujuan dengan kedalaman yang luar biasa: untuk beribadah kepada Allah dengan seluruh hidupnya, untuk menjadi khalifah-Nya di bumi dalam kedudukan khususnya, dan untuk berkontribusi pada perkembangan keluarga, komunitas, dan umat manusianya. Tujuan ini tidak berkurang oleh peran-peran spesifik yang ia tempati — istri, ibu, putri, profesional, anggota komunitas — tetapi diekspresikan melaluinya. Setiap perbuatan yang dilakukan dengan niat untuk menyenangkan Allah adalah ibadah; wanita Muslim yang benar-benar memahami ini dapat menemukan makna dalam tugas-tugas paling biasa dari kehidupan sehari-hari.

Al-Arifi membahas pentingnya memiliki tujuan dan proyek-proyek konkret — bukan sekadar tujuan umum menyenangkan Allah tetapi ambisi-ambisi spesifik dalam domain ilmu agama, kehidupan keluarga, pelayanan, dan pengembangan pribadi. Nabi ﷺ mengajarkan bahwa orang beriman harus mengejar kesempurnaan (itqan) dalam apa pun yang mereka lakukan, dan pengejaran kesempurnaan ini sendiri merupakan sumber kegembiraan. Wanita Muslim yang menetapkan tujuan-tujuan belajar — menghafal sebagian Al-Quran, mempelajari ilmu Islam tertentu, mengembangkan keterampilan yang memungkinkannya melayani komunitasnya dengan lebih efektif — dan kemudian mengejar tujuan-tujuan ini dengan disiplin dan tekad, akan merasakan kepuasan mendalam dari pencapaian yang tulus.

Pelayanan kepada orang lain — dakwah, pemberian sedekah, mendukung keluarga dan tetangga, berkontribusi pada proyek-proyek komunitas — adalah salah satu sumber paling andal dari jenis kegembiraan yang tidak cepat memudar. Penelitian psikologis tentang hal ini selaras persis dengan ajaran Islam: orang yang memberikan waktu, energi, dan sumber daya mereka dalam pelayanan kepada orang lain melaporkan tingkat kesejahteraan yang lebih tinggi daripada mereka yang berfokus terutama pada kebutuhan dan kesenangan mereka sendiri. Orientasi Islam terhadap pelayanan bukanlah beban tetapi resep kebahagiaan.

Al-Arifi membahas pengalaman umum merasa kewalahan — terutama bagi wanita Muslim yang mengelola peran-peran yang menuntut secara bersamaan. Ia tidak menawarkan fantasi kehidupan tanpa kewalahan tetapi justru alat-alat Islam untuk mengelolanya: lima shalat harian sebagai jeda-jeda terstruktur yang mengatur ulang perspektif dan prioritas seseorang, praktik mendelegasikan apa yang dapat didelegasikan, kebijaksanaan untuk membedakan antara apa yang esensial dan apa yang tampak mendesak, dan amalan spiritual menyerahkan apa yang di luar kendali seseorang kepada Allah dengan kepercayaan yang tulus.

Buku ini diakhiri dengan visi seorang Muslimah yang telah menyerap pelajaran-pelajarannya: seorang wanita yang memulai setiap hari dengan syukur dan mengingat Allah, yang mendekati hubungan-hubungannya dengan sabar dan cinta, yang mengejar tujuan-tujuannya dengan tekad dan kepercayaan pada dukungan Allah, yang memberikan waktu dan sumber dayanya dengan murah hati, yang menghadapi kesulitan dengan ketenangan orang yang tahu itu datang dari Yang Maha Bijaksana, dan yang membawa dalam dirinya pengetahuan yang mapan bahwa hidupnya memiliki tujuan tertinggi dan kisahnya memiliki akhir kegembiraan yang tertinggi. Wanita ini, dalam keyakinan al-Arifi, adalah benar-benar wanita paling bahagia di dunia — bukan karena keadaannya sempurna, tetapi karena ia telah menemukan sumber kebahagiaan yang tidak dapat dijangkau keadaan.
content_ar: The happiness that al-Arifi envisions for مسلم women is not passive but active — not a state to be waited for but a الحياة to be built. This concluding الباب brings together the threads of the preceding discussion into a vision of a purposeful, joyful مسلم woman's الحياة: one that is oriented toward الله, rich in relationships, productive in service, and resilient in the face of inevitable difficulties. Purpose is one of the most powerful contributors to human wellbeing, and الإسلام provides the مسلم woman with a purpose of extraordinary depth: للعبادة الله with her whole الحياة, to serve as His vicegerent on earth in her particular station, and to contribute to the flourishing of her الأسرة, الجماعة, and humanity. This purpose is not diminished by the specific roles she occupies — الزوجة, الأم, daughter, professional, الجماعة member — but expressed through them. Every act performed with the intention of pleasing الله is an act of worship; the مسلم woman who genuinely understands this can find meaning in the most ordinary tasks of daily الحياة. Al-Arifi discusses the importance of having concrete goals and projects — not merely the general aim of pleasing الله but specific ambitions in the domains of religious العلم, الأسرة الحياة, service, and personal development. النبي ﷺ taught that ينبغي للمؤمن pursue excellence (itqan) in whatever they do, and this pursuit of excellence is itself a source of joy. The مسلم woman who sets learning goals — memorizing a portion of القرآن الكريم, studying a particular Islamic science, developing a skill that allows her to serve her الجماعة more effectively — and then pursues these goals with discipline and determination, will experience the deep satisfaction of genuine accomplishment. Service to others — dawah, charitable giving, supporting الأسرة and neighbors, contributing to الجماعة projects — is one of the most reliable sources of the kind of joy that does not quickly fade. The psychological research on this point aligns precisely with Islamic teaching: people who give their time, energy, and resources in service of others report higher levels of wellbeing than those who focus primarily on their own needs and pleasures. الإسلام's orientation toward service is not a burden but a prescription for happiness. Al-Arifi addresses the common experience of feeling overwhelmed — particularly for مسلم women who are managing demanding roles simultaneously. He does not offer the fantasy of a الحياة without overwhelm but rather the Islamic tools for managing it: the five daily الصلوات as structured pauses that reset one's perspective and priorities, the practice of delegating what can be delegated, the الحكمة to distinguish between what is الجوهري and what merely appears urgent, and the spiritual practice of surrendering what is beyond one's control to الله with genuine trust. The الكتاب concludes with a vision of the Muslimah who has absorbed its lessons: a woman
